summary:
Priam's Treasure refers to a large cache of gold, silver, electrum, and copper objects discovered by Heinrich Schliemann at Hisarlik (ancient Troy) in 1873, found in the deposits corresponding to the [[event:1752|Troy II]] settlement phase, approximately 2450–2350 BC. The hoard included diadems, earrings, bracelets, vessels, and thousands of small gold objects — some of the finest goldsmithing from the Early Bronze Age Aegean world. Schliemann, certain he had found the legendary city of Homer's Iliad, named the cache after the Trojan king Priam and smuggled it out of the Ottoman Empire in violation of his excavation agreement.

Scholarly reassessment has established that the treasure predates the Trojan War (if it occurred at all as described) by approximately a thousand years, and thus cannot be associated with the Homeric characters. Its actual significance lies in what it reveals about Early Bronze Age wealth, craft skill, and trade networks in the Aegean and Anatolian world. The presence of gold (likely from Anatolia), silver, and electrum in such quantities at [[event:47|Troy]] confirms the site's role as a prosperous commercial centre with access to precious metals from surrounding regions.

The treasure's subsequent history has been turbulent. Schliemann gave a portion of the objects to his wife, who was famously photographed wearing the gold jewellery. The main body of the hoard was eventually donated to the Berlin Ethnological Museum, from which it was removed and hidden in a bunker during World War II, subsequently taken as war booty to Moscow, where it has been held at the Pushkin Museum since the late 1940s. Its ownership remains disputed between Germany, Russia, and Turkey.
